coredata.dc:title: One pot green synthesis of C-dots from groundnuts and its application as Cr(VI) sensor and in vitro bioimaging agent
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: V, Roshni
Misra, Sweta
Santra, Manas Kumar
Ottoor, Divya
coredata.dc:description: The emergence of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) has received special attention due to their distinct characteristics which are advantageous for optical sensing and bioimaging applications. Cr(VI) is considered a hazardous environmental pollutant hence requires a simple and effective method for its detection and recovery. Herein, we have synthesized both C-dots and N-doped C-dots from ground nuts employing a cost-effective and green hydrothermal method. N-C-dots exhibits a remarkably enhanced quantum yield (17.6%) as compared to undoped C-dots (7.8%). In addition,the N-C-dots showed amazing selectivity towards Cr(VI) in the metal sensing application. Cr (VI) induced quenching of fluorescence was recovered using Humic acid as well as Glutathione thus exhibiting an off-on type sensing mechanism. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) using N-C-dots was found to be 0.1 mg/L. C-dots possess comparatively low cytotoxicity than N-C-dots at higher concentration thereby making them an efficient candidate for bioimaging of MCF-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots(C-dots)
Groundnut
Cr(VI) sensing
Humic acid
Glutathione
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of humic acid (500 ppm) solution
Fluorescence off-on sensing using C. dot-Cr(VI)-Humic acid solution
Use of Glutathione for the reduction of Cr(VI) from C-dots-Cr(VI) solution
Effect of N-doping on the sensing ability of C-dots
Humic acid as a reducing agent for Cr(VI)
Effective use of Glutathione for the reduction of Cr(VI) from C-dots- Cr(VI) system
Bioimaging of MCF-7 cells using C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) U.V/Vis absorption spectra and (b) Emission spectra of C-dots when excited at wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm.
(a) TEM and (b) size distribution of C-dots produced by the hydrothermal irradiation.
(a) shows the photostability and (b) XRD spectra of C-dots of C-dots.
(a) TEM image and (b) I.R spectra of N-C-dots.
Effect of different metal ions on the emission intensity of the (a) C-dots and (b) N-C-dots respectively.

(a) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of N-Cdots and UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) (b) Quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) and a mixture of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) towards the N-C-dots.
shows the fluorescence response of N-C-dots on the increase in the concentration of Cr(VI) from 0.1 to 1 ppm.
The fluorescence enhancement of N-C-dots-Cr(VI) system by the addition of 1–10 ppm humic acid.
The fluorescence enhancement of N-C-dots-Cr(VI) system by the addition of 10 mM GSH (b) Enhancement in fluorescence intensity by adding 1–10 mM GSH and the Cr(VI) concentration was kept at 1 mM.
In vitro cell viability of MCF-7 cells treated with varying concentrations of C-dots for 24 h. as estimated by the MTT assay.
Confocal laser scanning images of MCF-7 cells upon incubation with C-dots for 24 h. (a) under bright ﬁeld (b) by excitation at 345 nm (c) 494 nm (d), and 589 nm.

head.ce:title: One pot green synthesis of C-dots from groundnuts and its application as Cr(VI) sensor and in vitro bioimaging agent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Savitribai Phule Pune University, Ganeshkhind Road, Pune, 411 007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Cell Science(NCCS), Pune, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emergence of fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) has received special attention due to their distinct characteristics which are advantageous for optical sensing and bioimaging applications. Cr(VI) is considered a hazardous environmental pollutant hence requires a simple and effective method for its detection and recovery. Herein, we have synthesized both C-dots and N-doped C-dots from ground nuts employing a cost-effective and green hydrothermal method. N-C-dots exhibits a remarkably enhanced quantum yield (17.6%) as compared to undoped C-dots (7.8%). In addition,the N-C-dots showed amazing selectivity towards Cr(VI) in the metal sensing application. Cr (VI) induced quenching of fluorescence was recovered using Humic acid as well as Glutathione thus exhibiting an off-on type sensing mechanism. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) using N-C-dots was found to be 0.1 mg/L. C-dots possess comparatively low cytotoxicity than N-C-dots at higher concentration thereby making them an efficient candidate for bioimaging of MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution is one of the adverse change brought about by the introduction of contaminants into the natural environment. Industrialization plays a crucial role in pollution by releasing waste materials containing heavy metal ions into the environment. Heavy metals are naturally occurring metals with high atomic weight and density. They have a wide application in industrial, domestic, agricultural, medical and technological fields [1–3]. But at the same time, the use of heavy metals leads to their excess distribution in the soil, air and aquatic system which causes harmful effects on flora and fauna [4]. Among these heavy metals, Chromium(Cr) has got special attention due to its toxic and carcinogenic nature. Some of the industries in which Chromium is widely used include metallurgy, textile, dye, leather tanning, photography [5,6] etc. Out of the various oxidation states of Chromium, Cr(III) and Cr(VI) species are prevalent in the environment. But these two species of Cr has entirely different properties. The Cr(VI) species are having high solubility and mobility which causes long-term adverse effects in humans as well as in animals [7,8]. In contrast, the Cr(III) species with less solubility and mobility is one of the micronutrients in humans and is less toxic [9,10]. According to WHO(World Health Organization) and BIS(Bureau of Indian Standards) the permissible limit of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 0.05 mg/l [11]. The current national and international news about the Cr(VI) contamination is alarming. Kanpur city of India discharges around 1500metric tons of chromium sulphate per annum as waste from its tanneries [12]. A health survey conducted by CSIR-IITR at Kanpur in 2012, has found digestive problems, dermatological disorders and hematological abnormalities in the communities of the surrounding area [13]. TamilNadu Chromates and Chemicals Limited(TCCL) in the Tamil Nadu state of India has dumped 2.27 lakh of chromate containing solid waste before it stopped operation in 1995 causing devastating effects in the environment and public health [14]. The Togupon river in Philippines is highly contaminated with Cr(VI) by the nickel processing plants in the surrounding area [15]. A case study of Cr(VI) pollution in Wu river in Taiwan was reported by Tseng et.al., where they evaluated the health costs of Cr(VI) exposure [16]. The above facts point towards the requirement of selective, sensitive and fast response analytical methods for the detection of Cr(VI) and suitable reagents for its conversion into less toxic Cr(III) which can be easily absorbed by the soil. The successful reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) has been reported earlierby Hu and co-workers, wherethey used CdS nanoparticles/nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide hybrid nanosheetsand BiVO4/Bi2S3 hollow discoid-like particles with mesoporous shell [17,18].

C-dots are carbonaceous nanoparticles with size below 10 nm. High photoluminescence, aqueous solubility, and nontoxicity are some of the unique characteristics which make C-dots superior compared to its semiconductor quantum dots [19]. The two main strategies for the synthesis of C-dots include Top-down and Bottom-up approaches out of which the latter has got much significance due to its simple and easy procedure. In the Bottom-up approach, carbonization of the precursors was carried out by methods like hydrothermal, microwave or thermal decomposition. Among them, the hydrothermal method oﬀ ;ers excellent means to produce C-dots with high quantum yield by having a good control over reaction conditions.

Use of fluorescent C-dots for the Cr(VI) detection has been experimented by Peng and co-workers where they employed phosphorous containing C-dots for the detection of Cr(VI) [20]. Zhang et. al., has used citric acid and thiourea as molecular precursors for the synthesis of C-dots which is further applied for the detection of Cr(VI) [21]. Liu and co-workershas used ILCD(Ionic liquids carbon dots)for the detection of chromium [22]. Shang and co-workers have used N-doped C-dots for the same [23]. Most of the above procedures require chemical precursors for the synthesis of C-dots, hence we target the development of green, non toxic and sensitive platform for Cr(VI) detection based on the C-dots synthesized purely from natural resources using greener method.

Application of non-cytotoxic and biocompatible C-dots synthesized from natural sources for bioimaging is worth investigating as these C-dots can be employed in medical diagnostic imaging. C-dots obtained from natural sources such as orange juice, milk, chitosan, coriander leaves etc. are experimented for bioimaging applications [24–27]

Selectivity is one of the significant parameter to be investigated in the development of a fluorescent sensor. Recently it was reported that the optical and electronic properties of heteroatom doped C-dots are far better than the bare C-dots [28]. The composition and structure of C-dots are found to be altered by the elemental doping also [29,30]. Doping C-dots with hetero atoms such as boron, nitrogen, phosphorous etc. can improve the properties of C-dots as they alter the electronic band structure and intrinsic properties of C-dots [31]. N doping is widely used because of the atomic size resemblance with carbon. The extra electrons got introduced by nitrogen doping can effectively change the optical and electronic properties of C-dots.

Herein, we report an extremely feasible and environmental friendly method for the synthesis of fluorescent C-dots from the natural and easily available groundnut for the detection of Cr(VI). This method demonstrates a unique and selective detection of Cr(VI) in presence of Cr(III) and the use of eco-friendly and naturally available Humic acid and Glutathione as a reducing medium for the conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The obtained non-cytotoxic C-dots were also used as a bioimaging agent for the imaging of MCF-breast cancer cells as breast cancer is the most common cancer that women community faces [32]. In order to increase the quantum yield and improve the selectivity of Cr(VI) detection, we have tried to modify the structure of C-dots by incorporating nitrogen into the system. There is an upsurge in the quantum yield and selectivity of C-dots towards Cr(VI)sensing was observed.

Groundnut, collected from the local market was washed and dried in a hot air oven at 50 °C. The skin was removed and the kernel, then powdered thoroughly for the hydrothermal treatment. The solutions of metal ions were synthesized from their respective chloride, sulphate or nitrate solutions. K2Cr2O7was used as the reagent for Cr(VI) analysis. All the reagents used for the metal analysis were purchased from SDFCL, and used without further purification. Ethylene diamine(EDA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Humic acid and Glutathione reduced was purchased from Hi-media Laboratories Pvt. Ltd, India. Triply distilled water was used as a solvent throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of C-dots were acquired on a Tecnai G2-20 Twin (FEI, Netherlands) transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Samples were prepared for analysis by evaporating a drop of aqueous solution of C-dots on a copper grid which was covered with a carbon film. The size distribution of C-dots was done by counting 50 particles. The absorbance and ﬂuorescence measurements were recorded using Shimdzu UV–vis spectrophotometer and Jasco FP-8300 SpectroFluorimeter (with 150 W Xe lamp), respectively. An Infrared spectrometer, (FTIR-8400, Shimadzu) was used to characterize the functional groups on the C-dots. The ﬂuorescence lifetime measurements were carried out using Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France, TCSPC instrument. X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of C-dots were obtained using a Rigaku Ultima IV powder X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.5A0 scanned in the range of 50–800 at a scan rate of 50/min. Confocal imaging was done using a NIKON A1 R Confocal Microscope. Raman Spectroscopy was done on a RENISHAW inVia Raman Microscope equipped with 632 nm laser source.

Groundnut(peanuts) is a legume crop grown mainly for its edible seeds. The seeds, as well as the groundnut oil, are used worldwide. Due to its high monounsaturated content, it is considered healthier than other saturated oils. It contains 25 g of protein in 100 g. They are rich in essential nutrients. Ina typical process, C-dots were prepared by the hydrothermal carbonization of groundnut powder. 1 g of groundnut was washed, dried and finely powdered and dissolved in 30 ml of distilled water. It was transferred into a 50 ml stainless steel autoclave and underwent hydrothermal treatment at 250 °C for 6 h. The black colored solution obtained was allowed to cool down and the large agglomerated particles were removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min. and filtered. The pale yellow colored supernatant solution obtained is lyophilized for 24 h. The obtained C-dots were dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 1 mg/10 ml(100 ppm)and is used for further analysis.

The quantum yield of C-dots was calculated by comparing their integrated fluorescence intensity with that of quinine sulphate. Quinine sulphate (Quantum yield = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 and C-dots were dispersed in water. The absorbance values of the two solutions were maintained at 0.1 at their excitation wavelengths to minimize the re-absorption eﬀ ;ects [33]. Excitation and emission slit widths were both set at 2.5 nm.

Cr(VI) stock solution (500 ppm) was prepared as follows: 0.28 g of K2Cr2O7was dissolved in 10 ml of water and makeup to 100 ml in a standard measuring flask. Cr(VI) solutions of 1–10 ppm and 10–100 ppm were prepared from the original stock solution by appropriate dilution method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of humic acid (500 ppm) solution
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of humic acid was prepared by dissolving 50 mg of material in 10 ml of 1 M NaOH solution followed by sonication and addition of 10 ml of 1 M HNO3 solution. The solution was diluted to 90 ml with triply distilled water and pH was adjusted to 7 by dropwise addition of 0.1 M NaOH. The solution was kept overnight and filtered. 1–10 ppm of the humic acid sample was used for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence off-on sensing using C. dot-Cr(VI)-Humic acid solution
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Cr(VI) was performed at room temperature in phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 7). In a typical run, 2 ml of C. dot solution(100 ppm) was added into 1 ml of phosphate buﬀ ;er, followed by the addition of a calculated amount of Cr(VI) ions(10 ppm) keeping the final volume as 5 ml. The fluorescent spectra were recorded after keeping the solution for 1 h at room temperature. A range of concentration of humic acid was added to the above solution in which C-dots and Cr(VI)concentrations were kept constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of Glutathione for the reduction of Cr(VI) from C-dots-Cr(VI) solution
ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of Cr(VI) using glutathione was performed in aqueous media where typically the concentration of C-dots and Cr(VI) were kept constant (1 mg/ml and 1 mM respectively) and a range of concentration of glutathione was added (1–10 mM).

MCF-7 (human breast cancer cell line) cells were maintained at 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator at 37 °C. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM)supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% streptomycin.

The biocompatibility and the suitability of C-dots for bioimaging were mainly determined by cytotoxicity study. In vitro cytotoxicity studies of C-dots obtained from ground nut was carried out on MCF-7 cells by MTT assay. Mitochondrial enzymes in live cells reduce soluble, yellow 3-(4,5-dimethythiazol2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) to an insoluble, purple formazan product. This insoluble product is then dissolved in a solvent and measured to assess the viability of cells. The cells were seeded in 96 well plates at a density of ˜5000 cells/ well. After 24 h of incubation the medium was replaced by different concentration of C-dots ranging from 0 to 1000 μg/ml (0, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000) for 24 h. After completion of incubation, cells were exposed to MTT solution (20 μl/well from the stock of 5 mg/ml) and incubated for next 3–4 h in humid 5% CO2 incubator. The solution in each well was now replaced with 100 μl/well MTT solvent [isopropanol, HCl (4 mM) and triton X-100 (0.01%)] and incubated in dark for 10–15 min at room temperature (with gentle shaking). The absorbance of the solution in each well was measured by Elisa plate reader (Thermo scientific MULTISKAN GO V3.2) at 590 nm. All experiments were carried out in biological duplicates. DMSO was used as the control in each experiment. The inhibition in MTT reduction of treated cells over control was evaluated for assessing the cytotoxicity of the C-dots on cancerous cells.
% cell viability = [OD590nmof treated cells / OD590nm of control cells] ×100

Bioimaging was performed in MCF-7 cells for which the cells were seeded in a density of 0.3 × 106 cells/ 35 mm plate (with coverslips) in a culture DMEM medium supplemented with 10% foetal bovine serum and 1%streptomycin. After 24 h, C-dots (1 mg/ml) was added to respective culture plates and incubated for next 24 h. Then the plates were washed with PBS twice for removing any unattached C-dots from the cell membrane. The respective coverslips were now mounted on glass slides and visualized under a confocal microscope for capturing fluorescent images.

C-dots obtained from the groundnut shows the characteristic behavior Similar to the previously reported C-dots from various sources [34]. The C-dots absorb mainly in the UV region which extends until the near-visible range as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The absorption band appears at 279 nm assigned to the π-π* (CC) transitions. It indicates the presence of conjugated double bonds and carbonyl groups on the surface of C-dots. A strong fluorescence emission peak is observed at 443 nm when excited at 360 nm, Fig. 1(b). The fascinating λex dependence of emission wavelength could be likely due to inhomogeneous size distribution or surface defects on the surface of C-dots. Generation of these defects creates complicated emissive traps on the C-dots which causes the excitons to gives rise emission during radiative recombination [35,36].

The Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM images of C-dots. The TEM image gives a direct observation of the size and morphology of the C-dots. The C-dots are found to be monodispersed with a spherical shape and are well separated in the solution. The size distribution is in the range of 2–8 nm with an average size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 2(b)). To probe the content and the chemical composition of the C-dots, the elemental analysis was done. From EDX analysis, the elemental composition of synthesized C-dots are found to be 92.56% carbon 2.42% nitrogen and 1.37% oxygen, Fig. S2(a).

The FT-IR results were used for identifying the characteristic functional groups on the C-dots. Fig. 3 indicate the presence of multiple functional groups on the surface of C-dots. The peak at 3250 cm−1 shows the stretching vibration of OH/NH which indicate the presence of a hydrophilic environment on the surface. The stretching vibration at 2929 cm−1 shows the presence of C–H bond. The peak at 1665 cm−1is the vibrational absorption of CO group and 1407cm−1is the bending vibration of C–H. The peak at 1583 cm−1 shows NH bending vibrations. The stretching modes of N−CH3 groups can be assigned at 1291 cm−1. The stretching vibration of C–O group is shown by 1033 cm−1. As per the above data, there might be the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on C-dots surface and these groups bestow a greater water solubility and stability to the C-dots which is favorable for applications of C-dots in the area of ﬂuorescence sensing.

The C-dots obtained from the groundnut shows good photostability as shown in Fig. 4 (a). The crystallinity of the C-dots was evaluated by the XRD measurement, and the result is shown in Fig. 4(b). The XRD pattern shows a broad amorphous hump at 2θ = 25.1° that are assigned to (002) planes of graphitic carbon which clearly indicates the amorphous and graphitic nature of C-dots.The lifetime measurement using a time-correlated single photon counting indicates an average lifetime of 2.76 ns shown in Fig. S3(a).The Raman spectrum of C-dots did not show any Raman signal under the given measurement conditions because of its strong ﬂuorescence background as shown in Fig. S5. The pH dependence of C-dots are shown in Fig. S3(b). A gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity is noted in the acidic and basic pH and the initial fluorescence intensity is retained at pH 7. Such pH-dependent behavior could be due to the changes in the surface state of C-dots due to the ionization of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. When going from acidic to neutral pH there may be the deprotonation of the carboxylic acid moiety to carboxylate ions thereby producing a negative environment on the surface of C-dot which in turn enhances the radiative recombination of electron and holes [37]. The ability to sustain the fluorescence intensity under constant irradiation enables the C-dots as a suitable candidate for bioimaging applications. The quantum yield of the obtained C-dots are found to be 7.87% as shown in Fig. S4(a)

The successful synthesis of C-dots from groundnut has led to the thought of using these aqueous soluble and highly fluorescent C-dots for metal sensing purpose. Recently, various research groups have been actively involved in the development of fluorescence sensors for selective and sensitive detection of metal ions [38–40]. In order to estimate the ability of C-dots obtained from groundnut for metal sensing purposes, their fluorescence intensity in the presence of various environmentally active metal ions was monitored and shown in Fig. 6(a). In this regard, 11metal ions were selected(each having 1 mM concentration). Out of this Hg2+, Fe3+displayed almost same fluorescence quenching like Cr(VI) due to its interaction with surface functional groups of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N-doping on the sensing ability of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Since the bare C-dots obtained from ground nut was lacking the selectivity for a particular metal ion the thought of modifying the C-dots evolved. N-doping provides the C-dots with remarkable properties, such as good quantum yield, improved catalytic activity and selective sensing ability toward metal ions [41,42]. The surface modification of the obtained C-dots using N-containing functional groups was found to be very lengthy and time-consuming [43]. Whereas, employing N-containing molecular precursors along with ground nuts during hydrothermal carbonization was found to be the effective and easiest method for N-doping. Here, EDA was used as an effective dopant in the stotiometric ratio of 2:1of C-dots and EDA respectively. During the hydrothermal method of synthesis, for the comparison of results with the bare C-dots, the amount of groundnut and the reaction conditions were maintained the same.

The TEM images of N-C-dots are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum, Fig. S1(a) the N-C-dots showed two observable absorption peaks at around 280 nm and 338 nm. The peak at 338 nm which was not obvious in bare C-dots can be due to n-π* transition of the amine groups which can be introduced by the addition of EDA into the system. The successful N-doping was confirmed by the EDX analysis(Fig. S2(b)) which shows an increase in N content from 2.42% in undoped C-dots to 13.01% in N doped C-dots.The I.R spectra of the N- doped C-dots are given in Fig. 5(b). Instead of a broad peak at 3250cm−1which is due to the overlapping of OH and NH groups, the obtainedN-C.dots shows a sharp and distinct peak at 3193 cm-1 indicating the presence ofmainly NH groups.The peak at 1670 cm−1showing the vibrational absorption of CO group showed a decrease in intensity compared to bare C.dots as shown in Fig. 3. The peaks at 1580cm−1 and1296 cm-1 indicating NH bending vibrations and the stretching modes of N−CH3 groups are more obvious in N-doped C.dots.All of the above observations suggesting that during the hydrothermal process the N atom has gradually substituted O in the C.dot core structure. The resultant N-C-dots obtained was having the quantum yield(17.6%) more than twice of the bare C-dots, (Fig. S4) and showed marvelously high selectivity towards Cr(VI) compared to other interfering metal ions, Fig. 6 (b)

The N-C-dots shows the excitation and emission peaks at 370 nm and 445 nm respectively. At the same time, the absorption spectra of Cr(VI) shows two intense peaks at 254 nm and 360 nm and less intense one at 448 nm as shown in Fig. 7 (a). The figure clearly depicts that Cr(VI) not only shields the excitation light of N-C-dots but also absorbs the emission intensity of C-dots. This might be attributed as the main reason for the quenching of fluorescence of N-C-dots by Cr(VI). The same reason for the quenching of fluorescence of N-C-dots synthesized from various sources by Cr(VI) has been pointed out by various literature reviews [44,45]. At the same time, the possibility of some charge transfer interaction between the N-C-dots and the metal ion which resulted in the nonradiative electron/ hole recombination cannot be ruled out. As earlier said, the two predominant oxidation states of Cr are (III) and (VI). From the Fig. 7(b) it is found that the N-C-dots obtained from the groundnut shows selective sensing towards Cr(VI)in presence of Cr(III).

To investigate the change in fluorescent properties of N-C-dots with varying concentration of Cr(VI), a series of concentration of Cr(VI) solution has been prepared and the change in fluorescent intensity is noted. For this experiment, Cr(VI) concentrations from 0.1 to 1 ppm has been prepared. As shown in Fig. 8 the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increase in the concentration of Cr(VI). Based on this, Stern-Volmer plot is generated and presented as shown in Fig. 9 .

The relationship between the fluorescent intensity of N-C-dots and concentration of Cr(VI) is given by Stern-Volmer equation, which can be written as,
(1) Fo/F = 1 + KSV[Q]--------------
Where Fo and F are fluorescence intensities of the N-C-dotsbefore and after the addition of metal ions. [Q] is the concentration of metal ions and Ksvis the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

For the above case, the S.V equation can be written as,
Fo/F = 1.0379 + 0.7292[Q]

A linear relation is obtained when the concentration of metal ion (Q) is plotted against F0/F value. The correlation coefficient (r) obtained was around 0.99. The slope of the line gives Ksv value, which was0.7292 ppm.The limit of detection(LOD) was found out using the (3σ)rule and its value was found to be0.1 ppm (1.9 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humic acid as a reducing agent for Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The removal of Cr(VI) as a pollutant from the environment is as important as its sensing. The convenient method of removal of Cr(VI) is its conversion to Cr(III) which is nontoxic and easily absorbable by soil. Various kinds of reducing agents have been used by researchers in this regard [46]. Here in this work, humic acid is used as an effective reducing agent to convert Cr(VI) to Cr(III). Humic acid is a natural reagent act as non-polluting conversion medium. The turn off-on system having N-C-dots-Cr(VI)-Humic acid has been tried never before.

The humic substance is a principal component of soil organic matter humus which contribute to soil physical and chemical quality [47]. They are dark brown, high molecular weight, heterogeneous materials that are obtained by the biodegradation of dead organic matter. These are polyfunctional macromolecules that contain a large number of different chemical functional groups like carboxylic, phenolic, polyphenolic and amine groups. Humic acids can associate strongly with organic and inorganic compounds in soil and water acting as both storage and transport agent [48].

The Fig. 10 shows the enhancement in the fluorescent intensity by the addition of humic acid into a system of N-C-dots-Cr(VI). Here, the N-C-dots(100 ppm) and Cr(VI)(10 ppm) were kept constant and the humic acid concentration was varied from 1 to 10 ppm. By eliminating the chance of absorption of excitation and fluorescence intensity of N-C-dots by Cr(VI) by converting it into Cr(III), N-C-dots-Cr(VI) system can effectively act as a turn-on sensor for humic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effective use of Glutathione for the reduction of Cr(VI) from C-dots- Cr(VI) system
ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) a naturally occurring antioxidant exists in both reduced (GSH) and oxidized (GSSG) states. A tripeptide, it has many functional groups like carboxyl, thiol and amino groups over it. In the reduced state, the thiol group of cysteine is able to donate electrons to other molecules thereby exhibiting good reducing property. With donating an electron, glutathione itself becomes reactive and readily reacts with another reactive glutathione to form glutathione disulphide (GSSG). Here, we incorporated this reducing property of GSH for the reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) from the N-C-dots- Cr(VI) system as shown in Fig. 11 . Removal of Cr(VI) using GSH is also acceptable from the green chemistry aspects and this conversion is found to be efficient in the neutral pH also.

Applicability of any system to be used as fluorescent imaging agent depends on its cytotoxicity. If the agent is cytotoxic to mammalian cells then it cannot be used for in vivo bioimaging. Owing to the cytotoxicity concerns it was essential to determine the cytotoxicity of C-dots synthesized from ground nuts. Thus, MTT assay was performed on MCF-7 cells which revealed negligible toxicity even at a concentration of 0.5 mg/ml. However, there was a slight decline in cell viability with the increasing concentration but still, the survival rate was 83% at a concentration of 1 mg/ml as shown in the graphical representation in Fig. 12 . This shows that the exposure of various concentration of C-dots did not make any serious toxicity on the MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of MCF-7 cells using C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The important characteristic properties of C-dots like its nanosize structure, high aqueous solubility, photostability, stability over biological pH, broad fluorescence tunability, appreciable cell viability etc. makes them a reliable choice for the bioimaging application. Confocal imaging of MCF-7 cells was performed to find out the feasibility of using C-dots for bioimaging. The cells were incubated with C-dots for 24 h at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. As the C-dots exhibit characteristic excitation-dependent emission behaviors, diﬀ ;erent colors of ﬂuorescence were observed by using different excitation wavelengths. As expected, blue, green, and red ﬂuorescence can be observed in the cell membrane and cytoplasm by excitation at 345, 494, and 589 nm, respectively, indicating that the C-dots were internalized by MCF-7 cells by endocytosis where they are mainly localized in the cell membrane and cytoplasm and very less penetrated into the nucleus as shown in the Fig. 13 . There was no apparent change in the morphology as well as the shape of the cells proving that the obtained C-dots from ground nuts can be used as an efficient bioimaging agent.

We have demonstrated a simple, natural and time-saving scheme for the synthesis of C-dots from groundnut via hydrothermal method. The obtained C-dots are highly fluorescent and having an average size of 2.5 nm. The same procedure was adopted for the synthesis of N- C-dots where EDA was used as N-dopant. The highly fluorescent N-C.doped were then effectively used for the selective detection of highly polluting and carcinogenic Cr(VI) through monitoring the quenching effect of fluorescence. The LOD is found to be 0.1 ppm (1.9 μM). Humic acid and Glutathione were used as a reducing agent to convert Cr(VI) to Cr(III) and thereby restoring the fluorescence of C-dots. Then, the noncytotoxic C-dots were effectively used for the bioimaging of MCF-7 cells.
The authors declare no conflicts

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) and the Department of Science and Technology (DST) New Delhi, India (SB/FT/CS-109/2012) and the authors are thankful to Dr. P. Biji, Associate Professor in Nanotechnology, Nanosensor Laboratory, PSG Institute of Advanced Studies and IISc Bangalore for TEM and TCSPC facilities respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.12.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
